EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across northern Utah during the morning of the 18th. Precipitation rapidly changed to snow down to the valley floors along the Wasatch Front. Widespread snow continued into the overnight hours, before tapering off early on the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front brought precipitation to northern Utah beginning on the morning of December 20th, and then a vigorous cold front moved through the state that afternoon and night. Snow showers, including a lake effect band, lingered following the front. A cold upper level low settled over northeast Utah on the 21st, and lake effect bands continued over Tooele Valley into the morning of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ben Lomond Peak received 18 inches of snow over a period of about 17 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An avalanche in a popular snowmobiling area killed one man.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 53 year-old man, who was snowmobiling with his family, was caught in an avalanche, buried by the snow, and died before being found. The avalanche occurred at a common spot for snowmobilers near Windy Ridge called Superbowl. No other injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a cold front that brought light snow to northern Utah, a lake-effect snow band formed over Tooele Valley and brought heavy snow there on the morning of December 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stansbury Park received 7 inches of snow from the lake-effect band off the Great Salt Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A number of storm systems impacted eastern Utah during the month but affects of long term dryness over the past several years allowed drought conditions to persist across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions ranging from moderate to severe continued to impact the area through the month. Impacts of the dryness were primarily hydrologic(water) in nature. For a continuation on this event please see the January 2008 Storm Data publication.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Massive Fire began June 29th near Neola, Utah and quicky spread due to extremely dry fuels and favorable weather conditions. Three civilian fatalities resulted from this fire. Over 40,000 acres had burned by July 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were 3 confirmed fatalities and over a dozen structures were destroyed by massive wildfires. Fatalities occurred in a field where farmers attempting to set up sprinklers were overcome by the fire. Two fatalities occurred on scene and the third occurred later at a hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Copious moisture aided in heavy thunderstorms developing over southwest Utah. Two to three inches of rain was estimated to have fallen in about an hour across Zion Park. One to two inches of rain fell from storms near St. George, causing street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm began on November 30th and continued into December. Please see the November 2007 Storm Data publication for additional information on this episode. A very moist subtropical flow of air spread across eastern Utah ahead of a powerful low pressure system. This produced widespread heavy snow across the mountains along with strong winds. Heavy snowfall also occurred across the lower elevations of northeast Utah, and heavy rainfall occurred across some of the lower elevations in southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of around 1 foot blanketed the area while frequent wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph caused blowing and drifting snow. A wind gust of 73 mph was recorded on top of La Sal Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three snowboarders were caught in an avalanche, leading to one fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three experienced snowmobilers were riding in Smith Basin, about 15 miles east-northeast of the Co-op trailhead, when an avalanche engulfed all three of them. Two of them were able to swim out of the snow, but the third, a 41 year-old male, was buried for at least 40 minutes, and died before they could dig him out. For the state of Utah, this was the third avalanche fatality in 8 days. It was also the 7th avalanche fatality for the 2007 calendar year, the most in one year since records have been kept, starting in 1958.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An avalanche in-bounds at the Canyons Resort caught 4 skiers in it, leading to three injuries and one fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 30 year-old male was killed in an avalanche at the Canyons Resort. The avalanche was triggered by two men who were descending upper Red Pine Chute;  one of the men was caught by the slide, but ended up on top of the snow.  The other man was caught and died of head trauma after hitting a tree.  A man and a child below were engulfed, with the man partially buried, and the child totally buried. The child was hospitalized for several days following the avalanche, but survived his injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very large Pacific storm brought heavy snow to most of the higher terrain and many of the valleys of Utah. Anywhere from 5 to 15 inches of snow fell in valley locations. The mountains received 1-2 feet with select locations receiving upwards of 3 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold storm closed out February by bringing 4 to 7 inches of snow to the valleys of Northern Utah and up to 1 1/2 feet in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northern Wasatch Front received 5 inches of snow in 6 hours, which created dozens of accidents on major roadways including I-15. Local media reported several temporary road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold storm closed out February by bringing 4 to 7 inches of snow to the valleys of Northern Utah and up to 1 1/2 feet in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Millcreek received 6 inches of snow, and heavy snow fell during the evening rush, resulting in over 100 traffic accidents in Salt Lake County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold storm closed out February by bringing 4 to 7 inches of snow to the valleys of Northern Utah and up to 1 1/2 feet in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of snow fell in 3 to 4 hours along the I-15 corridor, resulting in dozens of slide-offs and accidents. A heavy snow warning was issued based on impact to travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Subtropical moisture moved into Utah on November 30th ahead of a Pacific storm system that moved through the state November 30th into December 1st. This produced widespread snow across central and northern Utah, as well as heavy rain in southern Utah on the morning of December 1st. Note that this episode continued into December 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine inches of snow fell on the Bountiful Bench in 12 hours, with other sites in the area reporting 6 to 9 inches over the same time period. Note that this event continued into December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad trough was situated over the northern United States. Meanwhile, a strong shortwave trough embedded in northwest flow combined with a surge of moisture from the south to produce widespread snow and blowing snow across the mountains of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 10 inches accumulated across the La Sal and Abajo Mountains. Wind gusts of 20 to 35 mph caused blowing and drifting of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable snowpack resulted in an increased number of avalanches across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Human triggered avalanche on Signal Peak claimed the life of a snowmobiler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Natural avalanche killed a back country skier in Big Cottonwood Canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Avalanche killed a back country skier on Gobblers Knob in Big Cottonwood Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large winter storm brought mainly heavy snow to much of Utah. Accumulations ranges from 3 to 6 inches in many of the more populated valleys, to up to two feet in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alta Collins reported 15 in 12 hours. 12-18 inches were common across the Cottonwoods. The road to Little Cottonwood Canyon was closed for about 6 hours due to heavy falling snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy downpours from a tropical airmass resulted in washing out of streets and a few bridges in Washington County. A few roof evacuations were prompted by fast rising water in the town of Virgin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive flash flooding occurred in Zion National Park and areas outside of the park near the towns of Virgin and Touquerville. Due to the massive flash flood six homes were damaged and residents were evacuated off of their roofs. In addition, Kolob Terrace was closed.||Damage amounts were estimated from media reports (6 homes destroyed, $250K each; 3 homes damaged, $25K each; and a washed out bridge, $35K). In addition, the Zion RV Park reported damages totaling $205,000, not including loss of revenue.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the Red Canyon Water Treatment Plant and destroyed the transducer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Neola North Fire ignited during a period of hot and very dry conditions resulting in explosive fire growth. The blaze began in Duchesne County and quickly spread into Uintah County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Neola North Wildfire ignited during a period of hot and very dry conditions that resulted in explosive fire growth. Shortly after the blaze started it became deadly, rapidly sweeping across a field near the small community of Farm Creek near Whiterocks. The fire burned over and killed three people trying to protect property from the flames. The fire threatened many structures. Some structures were lost and  evacuations were ordered. The fire consumed lodgepole and pinyon pine, grass, brush, and spruce trees. This fire burned into the month of July and for a continuation on this incident please see the July 2007 Storm Data publication.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain producing thunderstorms caused flash flooding in Arches National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred along the Salt Wash in Arches National Park. Several inches of water was flowing over a temporary bridge built to replace the original foot bridge destroyed by flooding the previous year. The temporary bridge was damaged and was carried a few feet downstream. Water was also running across the road and parking lot in the Wolfe Ranch area of the Park. Water covered about 30 feet of the road and was up to 2 feet deep. The road leading to Delicate Arch was closed for a period of time as a precaution.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly intensifying thunderstorm moved across Daggett County and produced large hail in the Dutch John area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daggett County Sheriff's Office reported golf ball size hail near Dutch John and penny-sized hail at the Flaming Gorge Dam.  Minor street flooding was reported in Dutch John.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain producing thunderstorms occurred over eastern Utah and caused flooding in portions of Grand County and northwest San Juan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused street flooding in Castle Valley. Pea-size hail was mixed with the rain. Rainfall reports ranged from 1.63 to 2.90 inches in Castle Valley. At one location, 2.30 inches of rain fell within 45 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm produced damaging wind gusts in extreme northeast Utah.  A gust to 63 mph was measured at the Kings Point RAWS.  Numerous tree branches were broken off and unsecured items were blown hundreds of feet away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More flooding and wind damage across the state. Heavy rains ran across roadways and flooded a few parks in South Jordan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold spring storm system moved across eastern Utah and produced heavy snow and strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 6 inches occurred and frequent wind gusts of 20 to 35 mph caused blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal precipitation caused and increased in the dryness and drought conditions across eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lack of spring precipitation caused an increase in dryness to the point that Moderate Drought conditions developed across the eastern Uinta Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal precipitation caused and increased in the dryness and drought conditions across eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lack of spring precipitation caused an increase in dryness to the point that Moderate Drought conditions developed across the Tavaputs Plateau.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal precipitation caused and increased in the dryness and drought conditions across eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lack of spring precipitation caused an increase in dryness to the point that Moderate Drought conditions expanded across southeast Utah.  The exception was extreme southeast San Juan County where Severe Drought conditions had spread.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving spring storm system produced significant snowfall across the mountains of northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 5 to 7 inches occurred mainly along the north side of the eastern Uinta Mountains while 2 to 4 inches fell elsewhere.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold spring storm system moved across eastern Utah and produced heavy snow and strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 12 to 16 inches occurred across the La Sal Mountains while amounts around 5 inches fell across the Abajo Mountains.  Frequent wind gusts of 20 to 37 mph buffeted the area and caused blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across the Four Corners Area and produced significant snowfall across portions of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall of 3 to 5 inches occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across the Four Corners Area and produced significant snowfall across portions of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall of 5 to 6 inches occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe winds were produced with no damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 80 Dugway sensor measured a 62 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system brought strong winds to western Utah ahead of the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest recorded gust was 56 mph at Brimstone Reservoir RAWS, with estimated gusts 58 mph and above; sensors just across the zone border recorded gusts above 60 mph, and there are few sensors in this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system brought strong winds to western Utah ahead of the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milford Airport ASOS recorded a 63 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The effects of long term drought were still being felt across eastern Utah with moderate drought conditions plaguing much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite some autumn precipitation, the effects of long term drought continued to plague the area and severe to moderate drought conditions persisted. For a continuation on this drought situation please see the November 2007 Storm Data publication.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy, slow moving thunderstorms caused flooding in the Fremont river. Damage was reported in Capitol Reef National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash Flooding occured just south of the city center in Bloomington, with water, mud, and small boulders across the roadways. In addition, the storm drain was washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More flooding and wind damage across the state. Heavy rains ran across roadways and flooded a few parks in South Jordan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One foot deep water was reported on Redwood Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More flooding and wind damage across the state. Heavy rains ran across roadways and flooded a few parks in South Jordan.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 67 knot wind gust was recorded at the North Salt Flats mesonet site.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported trees and a power pole down on a vehicle near 600S 180E in Salt Lake City. No damage report was available.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy, slow moving thunderstorms caused flooding in the Fremont river. Damage was reported in Capitol Reef National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Sulphur Creek and the Fremont River. The primary damage was in Capitol Reef National Park. A number of stretches of SR 173 and other local roads were covered with mud and rocks, and a small bridge was washed out as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A microburst resulted in damage to a mobile home and a number of solar panels. The event occurred from a high-based thunderstorm with no precipitation or lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Microburst damage was reported by ABC TV in Salt Lake City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A day of high based thunderstorms brought severe microburst winds to northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 51 knot wind gust was measured at the sensor on Hat Island.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The I-80 Grassey sensor measured a 55 knot wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain once again produced a debris flow on the Salt Creek scar. In 15 minutes, 0.97 in rainfall was recorded at a local RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More flooding and wind damage across the state. Heavy rains ran across roadways and flooded a few parks in South Jordan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous power outages occurred across southern Salt Lake County due to flooded transformers. Roads were closed near 5000S 2700W and 7800S 300W with 2 feet deep water on the roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More flooding and wind damage across the state. Heavy rains ran across roadways and flooded a few parks in South Jordan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kimball burn scar received heavy rain, with mud and debris across SR 138.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bagder Springs RAWS in SW Utah received over an inch of rain in 20 minutes, and the dry washes were running in Ivins.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of high based thunderstorms produced gusty outflow winds across northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rosebud RAWS recorded a 55 knot wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of high based thunderstorms produced gusty outflow winds across northern Utah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy downpours from a tropical airmass resulted in washing out of streets and a few bridges in Washington County. A few roof evacuations were prompted by fast rising water in the town of Virgin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains brought flooding to parts of the Salt Lake Valley. A number of homes had water in basements and nearby parks were flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of 1.10 inches of rain occurred in 30 minutes in the southern end of the Salt Lake Valley. Basement flooding of 8 homes was reported by the media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains brought flooding to parts of the Salt Lake Valley. A number of homes had water in basements and nearby parks were flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A playground between South Jordan and Riverton was under water, with children swimming in the resultant pond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain in San Juan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain in the Cedar Point area in extreme east central San Juan County. 0.57 inches of rain was measured from the event, with most of the rainfall occurring in a 30 minute period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the Red Canyon Water Treatment Plant in the Uinta Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the Red Canyon Water Treatment Plant and destroyed the transducer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Neola North Fire ignited during a period of hot and very dry conditions resulting in explosive fire growth. The blaze began in Duchesne County and quickly spread into Uintah County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Neola North Wildfire ignited June 29th during a period of hot and very dry conditions that resulted in explosive fire growth. Shortly after the blaze started it became deadly, rapidly sweeping across a field near the small community of Farm Creek near Whiterocks. The day the fire started it burned over and killed three people trying to protect property from the flames. The fire threatened many structures. Some structures were lost and evacuations were ordered. The fire consumed lodgepole and pinyon pine, grass, brush, and spruce trees. The fire burned approximately 43,511 acres before being contained on July 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heay rain occurred in the Moab and Arches National Park areas resulting in minor flooding in Moab and flash flooding in Arches National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused 6 to 12 inches of water to flow across the road near Wolfe Ranch in Arches National Park as Salt Wash came out of its banks.  The road was temporarily closed. Flooding also occurred in the visitor center area of Arches National Park where water entered the main visitor center building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heay rain occurred in the Moab and Arches National Park areas resulting in minor flooding in Moab and flash flooding in Arches National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and debris caused storm sewers to clog in Moab that resulted in minor street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long-term dryness and above normal temperatures kept moderate to severe drought conditions across eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southwest monsoon brought surges of moisture and periodic outbreaks of showers and thunderstorms to eastern Utah. However, moderate to severe drought conditions and water supply concerns remained across east-central Utah due to long-term dryness and above normal temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long-term dryness and above normal temperatures kept moderate to severe drought conditions across eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southwest monsoon brought surges of moisture and periodic outbreaks of showers and thunderstorms to eastern Utah. However, moderate to severe drought conditions and water supply concerns remained across southeast Utah due to long-term dryness and above normal temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm began on November 30th and continued into December. Please see the November 2007 Storm Data publication for additional information on this episode. A very moist subtropical flow of air spread across eastern Utah ahead of a powerful low pressure system. This produced widespread heavy snow across the mountains along with strong winds. Heavy snowfall also occurred across the lower elevations of northeast Utah, and heavy rainfall occurred across some of the lower elevations in southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of around 1 foot blanketed the area while frequent wind gusts of 25 to 45 mph caused blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist subtropical flow of air spread across eastern Utah ahead of a powerful low pressure system. This produced widespread heavy snow across the mountains along with strong winds. Heavy snowfall also occurred across the lower elevations of northeast Utah, and heavy rainfall occurred across some of the lower elevations in southeast Utah.|This episode continued into the following month. For additional information please see the December 2007 Storm Data Publication.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of around 1 foot blanketed the area while frequent wind gusts of 25 to 45 mph caused blowing and drifting snow. For a continuation on this event please see the December 2007 Storm Data publication.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long-term dryness and above normal temperatures kept moderate to severe drought conditions across eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southwest monsoon brought surges of moisture and periodic outbreaks of showers and thunderstorms to eastern Utah. However, moderate to severe drought conditions and water supply concerns remained across southeast Utah due to long-term dryness and above normal temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions showed some improvement across eastern Utah as a series of Pacific storm systems brought substantial rainfall to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month began with severe drought conditions, except for a sliver of moderate drought conditions along the Colorado border.  By the end of the month, the moderate drought area had improved to abnormally dry conditions, and the severe drought area had improved to moderate drought condtions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions showed some improvement across eastern Utah as a series of Pacific storm systems brought substantial rainfall to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Conditions improved during the month from severe drought to moderate drought by the end of the month in the eastern Uinta Basin.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions showed some improvement across eastern Utah as a series of Pacific storm systems brought substantial rainfall to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions on the Tavaputs Plateau improved from severe drought to moderate drought or abnormally dry conditions by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions showed some improvement across eastern Utah as a series of Pacific storm systems brought substantial rainfall to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month began with moderate to severe drought conditions.  By the end of the month, the moderate drought areas improved to abnormally dry conditions, and the severe drought areas improved to moderate drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from strong thunderstorms in northeast Utah produced flash flooding in the Clay Basin area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flash flood with water at least five feet deep ran down an arroyo and across Browns Park Road, about three miles east of the intersection of Red Creek and Browns Park Road.  There was some erosion damage to the road and a section of wood fence upstream was torn out and carried down to the road.  Road crews repaired the road and cleared out debris from the culverts the next day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions showed some improvement across eastern Utah as a series of Pacific storm systems brought substantial rainfall to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Conditions improved during the month from severe drought to moderate drought by the end of the month in the eastern Uinta Mountains.  The exception was a small portion of Daggett County near the Wyoming border which remained in severe drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall occurred around the town of Eastland in far east central San Juan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.20 inches occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous autumn weather system ushered in the coldest air of the season so far.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures reached 31 to 32 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions showed some improvement across eastern Utah as a series of Pacific storm systems brought substantial rainfall to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions prevailed early in the month, except for a sliver of moderate drought conditions along the Colorado border.  By the end of the month, the moderate drought area had improved to abnormally dry conditions, and the severe drought area improved to moderate drought conditions.||||Moderate drought to abnormally dry conditions plagued the La Sal and Abajo Mountains through the month. However, some improvement occurred as severe drought diminished.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions showed some improvement across eastern Utah as a series of Pacific storm systems brought substantial rainfall to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month began with widespread severe drought conditions in the Canyonlands and Natural Bridges area of southeast Utah. By the end of the month, the situation had improved to moderate drought conditions with only a few lingering patches of severe drought.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Upper Avenues, large tree limbs were knocked down. Wind gusts of 55-60 mph were common across the Salt Lake Valley including the University of Utah. There were also numerous reports of small tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front brought precipitation to northern Utah beginning on the morning of December 20th, and then a vigorous cold front moved through the state that afternoon and night. Snow showers, including a lake effect band, lingered following the front. A cold upper level low settled over northeast Utah on the 21st, and lake effect bands continued over Tooele Valley into the morning of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In far southern Utah, thunderstorms developed from this system. The St. George AWOS recorded wind gusts associated with one of these thunderstorms of over 61 miles per hour on three straight observations, at 855, 915, and 935 MST.
